coredata.dc:title: Dual-emission fluorescence sensor based on biocompatible bovine serum albumin stabilized copper nanoclusters for ratio and visualization detection of hydrogen peroxide
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Cunjin
Yang, Min
Mi, Guohua
Zhang, Bin
Dou, XiuHong
Liu, Enzhou
Hu, Xiaoyun
Xue, Weiming
Fan, Jun
coredata.dc:description: In this work, bovine serum albumin stabilized copper nanoclusters (BSA-CuNCs) with highly red fluorescent were fast prepared by increasing the temperature in aqueous medium and used as a fluorescent probe for the sensitive detection of H2O2 in water and rat serum. Unexpectedly, the prepared BSA-CuNCs showed dual emission peaks at 450 and 620 nm, respectively, and had excellent stability such as outstanding photobleaching resistance and salt tolerance. Under optimized conditions, H2O2 can reduce the fluorescence signal at 620 nm (F620) and enhance the fluorescence signal at 450 nm (F450), meanwhile, the color of the probe changed from red to blue, resulting in ratiometric and visualization detection of H2O2 with high accuracy. F450/F620 was linearly proportional to H2O2 concentration in the ranges from 0 to 100 μM, with limit of detection 0.082 μM. In the rat serum spiked experiment, it showed a reasonable recovery rate. More importantly, cytotoxicity tests showed that BSA-CuNCs had little cytotoxicity. Therefore, the prepared BSA-CuNCs fluorescent probe has potential practical application prospects.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red fluorescent copper nanoclusters
Ratio fluorescent probe
Detection of hydrogen peroxide
Visualization detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the BSA-CuNCs
The ratiometric fluorescence probe detection H2O2 based on BSA-CuNCs
Detection of H2O2 in actual samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22078261 and 11974276 ), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2016M600809 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of the as-prepared BSA-CuNCs, inset is the size distribution histogram; (b) HRTEM images (illustration for partial enlargement) of BSA-CuNCs; (c) XRD patterns of BSA and BSA-CuNCs.
(a) XPS wide survey spectrum of BSA-CuNCs; (b) The Cu 2p amplified XPS of BSA-CuNCs.

(a) UV absorption of BSA and BSA-CuNCs in aqueous solution, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra, inset are photographs of BSA-CuNCs under sunlight (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right). (b) Fluorescence decay curves of BSA-CuNCs and BSA-CuNCs/H2O2.

(a) When the excitation wavelength is 360 nm, the fluorescence emission intensity of BSA cuncs solution with different pH values at 450 nm or 620 nm; (b) Fluorescence photos of BSA-CuNCs in different pH solution; (c) The fluorescence spectra of BSA cuncs solution at pH 6-5 under 360 nm excitation; (d) Fluorescence photos of BSA-CuNCs solution at pH 6-5.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of BSA-CuNCs solution excited by 360 nm at different times after adding 100 μM hydrogen peroxide; (b) Fluorescence intensity F450/F620 of BSA-CuNCs solution at different times after adding 100 μM hydrogen peroxide.

(a) The fluorescence emission spectra of BSA-CuNCs solution with increasing H2O2 concentrations (0–100 μM) under 360 nm excitation; (b) Linear relationship between F450/F620 versus H2O2 concentrations in the range of 0–100 μM; (c) The fluorescence photos of BSA-CuNCs with versus H2O2 concentrations in the range of 0–100 μM under 365 nm UV lamp; (d) The Cu 2p amplified XPS of BSA-CuNCs/H2O2.
Viability of HeLa cells followed by MTT assay after 20 h treatment with BSA-CuNCs.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of dual-emission BSA-CuNCs.
Fluorescence lifetimes of BSA-CuNCs and BSA-CuNCs/H2O2.
The method was compared with other reported fluorescence methods for determination of H2O2.
Recoveries of H2O2 in spiked rat serum samples.

head.ce:title: Dual-emission fluorescence sensor based on biocompatible bovine serum albumin stabilized copper nanoclusters for ratio and visualization detection of hydrogen peroxide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710069, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an, 710069, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710127, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics, Northwest University, Xi’an, 710127, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710069, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an, 710069, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710069, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Sciences, Northwest University, Xi’an, 710069, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, bovine serum albumin stabilized copper nanoclusters (BSA-CuNCs) with highly red fluorescent were fast prepared by increasing the temperature in aqueous medium and used as a fluorescent probe for the sensitive detection of H2O2 in water and rat serum. Unexpectedly, the prepared BSA-CuNCs showed dual emission peaks at 450 and 620 nm, respectively, and had excellent stability such as outstanding photobleaching resistance and salt tolerance. Under optimized conditions, H2O2 can reduce the fluorescence signal at 620 nm (F620) and enhance the fluorescence signal at 450 nm (F450), meanwhile, the color of the probe changed from red to blue, resulting in ratiometric and visualization detection of H2O2 with high accuracy. F450/F620 was linearly proportional to H2O2 concentration in the ranges from 0 to 100 μM, with limit of detection 0.082 μM. In the rat serum spiked experiment, it showed a reasonable recovery rate. More importantly, cytotoxicity tests showed that BSA-CuNCs had little cytotoxicity. Therefore, the prepared BSA-CuNCs fluorescent probe has potential practical application prospects.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) plays significant roles in human health and life that adjusts various physiological events in living organisms [1]. The abnormal concentration of H2O2 in the body can cause a certain degree of damage to the body [2]. For instance, normal levels of H2O2 in intracellular are relevant to the adjustment of cell propagation and growth arrest. In contrast, significantly inflated concentrations of H2O2 can cause oxidative damage of cellular proteins and provoke cellular decrepitude and apoptosis [3,4]. Additionally, H2O2 regularly be regarded as a carcinogen [5,6]. Many traditional techniques including titration, spectrophotometry, phosphorescence spectrum, chromatography and electrochemical have been used to detect the contents of H2O2 of vivo or vitro [7,8]. However, due to laborious pretreatment, sluggish processes, and high cost, traditional methods for determining the concentration of H2O2 in environmental and biological samples cannot be widely used in actual tests. Therefore, a simple, fast and sensitive method for detecting H2O2 should be urgently developed. In the past few years, fluorescent probe technology has been widely reported in the field of analysis and detection due to its simple operation, low cost and high efficiency [9,10]. These include fluorescent probes based on traditional fluorescent dyes and fluorescent nanomaterials. Compared with fluorescent dyes, fluorescent nanomaterials have been widely used in display and detection because of their strong anti-photobleaching performance, adjustable fluorescence, and simple preparation process [11–13].

In recent years, there are some reports on the detection of H2O2 with fluorescent nanomaterials. Still, most of them involved the heavy metal ion Cd2+, which was harmful to the body, and the prepared fluorescent probes were single emission signals [14–16]. However, the single emission fluorescent probes are easily affected by the external environment such as light source, photobleaching and probe concentration. Due to its self-calibration function, the dual emission ratio fluorescent probe can avoid the interference of the external environment and has higher sensitivity and selectivity. At present, increasing number of ratio fluorescent probes were used to detect small molecules and ions, but most ratio fluorescent probes used for H2O2 detection were based on fluorescent dyes and required convoluted producing process and high production cost [17–19]. Therefore, it is necessary to explore ratio fluorescent nanoprobes with simple preparation process and low production cost for H2O2 detection.

Nowadays, metal nanoclusters (MNCs), which composed of several to tens of atoms, have become promising fluorescent probes for the small molecule identification and cells imaging, which is due to their unique superiority such as low toxicity, large Stokes shift, excellent resistance to photobleaching and marvelous biocompatibility compared with organic dyes and toxic quantum dots [20–22]. Among the metal nanoclusters such as AuNCs, Ag NCs and CuNCs, CuNCs have attracted much attention owing to their excellent optical properties and copper as a relatively cheap, abundant and readily available element [23,24]. For example, ovalbumin-stabilized CuNCs with red fluorescence were used for label-free detection of folic acid [25]; l-cysteine stabilized fluorescent CuNCs were used for sensitive detection of mercury ions [26]; Thiolate-protected CuNCs with aggregation-induced emission was used for β-galactosidase activity monitoring [27]. However, although there are many reports on copper nanoclusters, there are few reports on the one pot preparation of double emission copper nanoclusters ratio fluorescent probes.

In the present study, a low-cost, simple and one-pot rapid preparation of red double-emitting biocompatible copper nanoclusters (BSA-CuNCs, F450 and F620) ratio fluorescent probe based on bovine serum albumin stabilized were reported. The fluorescence of BSA-CuNCs was sensitive to pH and H2O2. As the concentration of H2O2 increased from 0 to 100 μM, the intensity of F450 gradually increased, and at the same time, the intensity of F620 gradually decreased, causing the visible fluorescence of BSA-CuNCs solution gradually changed from red to blue. The emission signal of BSA-CuNCs has the same characteristics as H2O2 for pH 6-5. Therefore, the prepared BSA-CuNCs dual emission ratio fluorescent probes were used for the sensitivity and visual detection of H2O2 and pH.

Bovine serum albumin, Fraction V (BSA) was obtained from Kehao Biological Engineering Co., Ltd. Copper chloride and H2O2 30% were purchased at Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Rhodamine B was purchased from Beijing Chemical Plant. Phosphate buffered solution (pH 7.2–7.4, 0.01 M) was purchased from Quanzhou Hongke Biotechnology Co., Ltd. HeLa cell line and Rat serum were provided by Shaanxi University of Traditional Chinese Medicine. Thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide (MTT) was obtained from Solarbio Life Sciences. Dimethyl sulfoxide was purchased from Tianjin kemeo Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Reagents for interference detection were placed in the Electronic Supplementary Information section. All the reagents were analytical grade and had not undergone further purification. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiment process.
The instruments information of work was placed in the Electronic Supplementary Information.

For synthesis of BSA-CuNCs, 200 mg BSA was dissolved in 50 mL H2O, then 0.5 mL CuCl2 solution (0.1 M) was added into BSA solution, stirring for 10 min. Afterwards, 0.6 mL hydrazine hydrate (80%) was slowly added into the above mixed solution when temperature reached to 70 °C. After 30 min reaction, the solution becomes transparent bright yellow. After the temperature dropped to room temperature, the large particles were removed through centrifuging at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. In order to further purify to remove unreacted small molecule impurities, the supernatant was dialyzed with a 2000 D dialysis bag for 48 h. Finally, a yellow BSA-CuNCs solution was obtained and stored at room temperature for further use.

The quantum yield (QY) of BSA-CuNCs was obtained in reference to rhodamine B (QY = 97%). The quantum yield of BSA-CuNCS was calculated using equation (1),
(1)
ϕ x
=
ϕ s
×
A s
A x
×
I x
I s
×
η x
2
η s
2
Where
ϕ
,
A
,
I
, and
η
expressed the quantum yield, absorbance, integral area of target fluorescence intensity and refractive index of the solvent, respectively. The subscript
s
and
x
indicated the reference and object, respectively.

The pH of BSA-CuNCs solution was tuned by NaOH solution (1 M) and HCl (1 M), The environment pH condition of BSA-CuNCs was controlled between 14 and 1, and then the fluorescence spectra were measured and recorded.

A typical H2O2 detection procedure was conducted as follows. The BSA-CuNCs concentration is 100 μg/mL. Different concentrations of H2O2 stock solutions (0.5 mL) were added to 4.5 mL as-prepared BSA-CuNCs (100 μg/mL), and the final concentration of H2O2 was 0–100 μM, followed by incubation at room temperature for 2 min, and then the fluorescence spectra of these solutions were measured and recorded.

The cytotoxicity study of BSA-CuNCs was performed on HeLa cell by a standard MTT assay. The HeLa cell was cultured in complete growth medium containing 90% RPMI 1640 medium, 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin/streptomycin. Culture conditions were 37 °C, 5% CO2 incubator, 2–3 days of liquid change or passage. The cultured HeLa cells were subcultured into 96 well plates (1 × 105 cells/well) and cultured in incubator with 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 20 h. Then the supernatant was discarded and the control group and the treated group were set up respectively. The control group was added with 100 μL medium, and the treated group was added with 90 μL medium and 10 μL BSA-CuNCs of different concentrations, and then cultured in the incubator with 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 20 h. Then 20 μL MTT reagent (5 mg/mL) was added into each well, and the supernatant was discarded after 4 h of culture. Each well was dissolved with 150 μL DMSO. After shaking for 5 min, the absorbance was detected at 490 nm. The cell viability was calculated according to the following equation:
(2)
Cell viability
( % )
=
Absorbance treated
Absorbance control
× 100 %
Three parallel tests were conducted out in three generations of cells to prove the credibility of the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the BSA-CuNCs
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, BSA-CuNCs with red fluorescence were synthesized via a facile and fast approach using low-cost raw materials such as copper chloride, BSA and hydrazine hydrate. The preparation process of BSA-CuNCs were shown in Scheme 1 . BSA with excellent biocompatibility acted as a stabilizer of BSA-CuNCs, and its functional group such as –NH2, –SH, –OH can though coordination interactions with Cu2+ form complexation [28]. In order to obtain BSA-CuNCs with exceptional fluorescence, the experimental conditions were optimized, including the reaction temperature, reaction time and the ratio of BSA and hydrazine hydrate to Cu2+. Generally speaking, the temperature was particularly important for the reaction process, and increasing temperature can accelerate the reaction process. As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescence intensity of reaction at 70 °C for 30 min was almost equivalent to that of reaction at room temperature for 4 h. As shown in Table S1, the preparation time of CuNCs that have been reported up to several hours [25,26,28–36], compared with this, the method provided by this work greatly reduces the preparation cost. As shown in Fig. S2a, the optimal reaction temperature was 70 °C, exceeding 70 °C, which may cause aggregation or more surfaces defects of CuNCs and lead fluorescence quenching. Under the reaction temperature of 70 °C, the fluorescence spectra of BSA-CuNCs prepared under different reaction times were compared. As shown in Fig. S2b, with the increased of time, the increasing number of BSA-CuNCs were produced, and the surface of BSA-CuNCs was more complete, so the fluorescence of that gradually increased. When the reaction time exceeded 30 min, aggregation or surface defects of BSA-CuNCs might occur, which reduced the fluorescence intensity. As a stabilizer, the dosage of BSA was essential for the preparation of high-performance BSA-CuNCs. As shown in Fig. S2c, with the increased of BSA content, the fluorescence intensity of BSA-CuNCs reached the highest point, because more BSA can increase the concentration of BSA-CuNCs. However, when the amount of BSA exceeded 200 mg, the fluorescence intensity decreased, which may be caused by the huge spatial effect of excessive BSA [37]. The copper in BSA-CuNCs was zero-valent, therefore, a reducing agent was required to reduce Cu2+ to Cu0. Although BSA had reducibility, it was too weak to achieve the purpose of rapid preparation of BSA-CuNCs, so used a strong reducing N2H4·H2O acted as a reducing agent. As shown in Fig. S2d, when there was no N2H4·H2O, the product only had an emission peak at 450 nm. When N2H4·H2O was added, a new emission peak appeared at 620 nm, which proved the formation of BSA-CuNCs. As the amount of N2H4·H2O increased, the emission peak at 450 nm gradually decreased, while the emission peak at 620 nm gradually strengthened. When the amount was 0.6 mL, the changing trend was no longer obvious, because all Cu2+ had been reduced to Cu0, and the concentration of CuNCs reached the maximum. Therefore, it was decided to use 0.6 mL N2H4·H2O as the best reaction condition. In order to explore the source of fluorescence at 450 nm, the fluorescence spectra of BSA solution and Cu(II)-BSA mixed solution after stirring for 10 min were measured. As shown in Fig. S3a, BSA and Cu(II)-BSA solution had a strong emission peak at 450 nm, and the FWHM of Cu(II)-BSA was narrower and the intensity was higher due to Cu2+ complexation. More importantly, there was no Cu(II)-BSA in BSA-CuNCs because Cu2+ had been completely reduced by hydrazine hydrate. Therefore, it can be proved that the emission peak at 450 nm of BSA-CuNCs originated from the self fluorescence of BSA. The results showed that the final reaction process for preparing BSA-CuNCs was summarized as: 0.5 mL CuCl2 (0.1 M) solution, 200 mg BSA, 0.6 mL N2H4·H2O, the reaction temperature was 70 °C, and the reaction time was 30 min.

The microstructure and chemical composition of BSA-CuNCs were characterized by TEM, XRD, FTIR and XPS. In Fig. 1 a, the TEM image of BSA-CuNCs exhibited a spherical morphology, a narrow size distribution of 1.53–3.82 nm with an average particle size of 2.62 nm based on analysis of 81 particles. Further, the HRTEM (Fig. 1b, inset) of BSA-CuNCs revealed the clear lattice fringes with lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, corresponding to the (111) plane of metal Cu [38]. The XRD spectrum of BSA-CuNCs (Fig. 1c) also proved the existence of (111) crystal planes of Cu0 (HCPDS 89–2838) [39]. However, the diffraction peaks of CuNCs cannot be clearly observed, which may be ascribed to masked by the diffraction peak of BSA. FTIR analysis (Fig. S3b) showed that BSA-CuNCs had almost the same functional group as BSA. The characteristic peaks at 3280, 1631 and 1514 cm−1 were consistent with the stretching vibration of –OH, CO and COO- of –COOH, respectively, and these functional groups would give the BSA-CuNCs good water solubility [28,40]. The XPS was used to analyze the ingredients and chemical states of element in BSA-CuNCs. Survey spectrum (Fig. 2 a) of BSA-CuNCs indicated the presence of Cu 2p (0.19%), C1s (68.78%), O 1s (16.79%), N 1s (12.39%) and S 2p (1.86%). The Cu 2p spectrum (Fig. 2b) showed two peaks at 933.38 eV and 952.88 eV corresponding to the Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2 [15]. This indicates the existence of Cu (0) and Cu (I) [41]. No satellite peak at around 943.0 eV confirms that there was no Cu (II) in BSA-CuNCs [42].

As shown in Fig. 3 a, UV absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of samples were used to characterize the optical properties of BSA-CuNCs. BSA had a sharp absorption band at 276 nm, and BSA-CuNCs had a characteristic absorption band located at 270 nm, which was consistent with previous reports [28]. In the region of 300–590 nm, the excitation spectrum of BSA-CuNCs was detected, and the maximum of excitation center was located at 360 nm. Therefore, 360 nm was chosen as the optimal excitation position. Upon the best excitation wavelength of 360 nm, BSA-CuNCs exhibited two emission peaks, located at 450 nm and 620 nm, respectively. The emission peak at 450 nm was attributed to the inherent fluorescence emission of BSA, and the emission at 620 nm was ascribed to the triplet emission of CuNCs [43]. The lifetime of triplet emission fluorescence is usually in the microsecond range [44,45]. The fluorescence decay curves of BSA-CuNCs and BSA-CuNCs/H2O2 at 620 nm emission were measured to verify the fact of triplet emission. The experimental results were shown in Fig. 3b, and the lifetime data were summarized in Table 1 . Herein, the fluorescence decay curves of BSA-CuNCs was fitted by three-exponential function (2).
(3)
R
( t )
=
B 1
e
− t /
τ 1
+
B 2
e
− t /
τ 2
+
B 3
e
− t /
τ 3
Where
R
( t )
indicated the fluorescence intensity and
t
represented time, the weighted mean lifetime τav was calculated according to equation (3).
(4)
τ
a v
=
B 1
τ 1
+
B 2
τ 2
+
B 3
τ 3
B 1
+
B 2
+
B 3
Where
τ i
(i = 1, 2, and 3) were the lifetimes of shorter- or longer-lived species and
B i
(i = 1, 2, and 3) were their respective amplitudes, and χ2 was the goodness of the fit. The fluorescence lifetime for BSA-CuNCs and BSA-CuNCs/H2O2 were calculated to be about 2.201 μs and 1.900 μs, respectively. The result was consistent with the fact of triplet emission. Because the electrons emitted from the triplet state undergo forbidden transitions, the quantum yield is usually tiny. Using rhodamine B as a standard reference, the quantum yield of the emission peak of BSA-CuNCs at 620 nm was 2.32%. The emission peaks of BSA-CuNCs at different excitation wavelengths were investigated. As shown in Fig. S4a, when the excitation wavelength was shifted from 320 nm to 400 nm, the emission peak of 450 nm shifted from 423 nm to 474 nm, and the intensity of emission peak first rose and then decreased. However, the emission peak position of 620 nm did not move, but the intensity changed. The intensities of the two emission peaks both reached their maximum under excitation at 360 nm. In order to investigate the excitation dependence of BSA-CuNCs at 450 nm, the emission of pure BSA solution and Cu(II)-BSA solution was recorded at different excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. S4b and Fig. S4c, BSA solution and Cu(II)-BSA solution had the same phenomenon of excitation dependence as BSA-CuNCs. Therefore, the excitation dependence of BSA-CuNCs at 450 nm may be related only to BSA with different functional groups, but not to the generated CuNCs. The emission at 620 nm independent on the excitation wavelength, which is similar to the optical properties of semiconductor quantum dots. The excitation independence and large stokes shift indicated that the emission at 620 nm originates from CuNCs rather than fluorescent dyes.

The photochemical stability and colloidal stability of BSA-CuNCs are crucial for the application of BSA-CuNCs in the actual environment. Therefore, the photobleaching resistance, salt-resistance and acid and alkali resistance of BSA-CuNCs were investigated. The experimental results showed that the fluorescence intensity of the BSA-CuNCs solution not only under UV irradiation for up to 10 h (Fig. S5a), but also in the NaCl solution with a concentration as high as 1 mol/L (Fig. S5b) did not change significantly. Therefore, BSA-CuNCs dual emission fluorescence signal had the potential for uninterrupted tracking studies.Fig S6 further showed BSA-CuNCs had no apparent response to numerous molecules and ions (1 mmol/L) except H2O2.

Based on previous reports, pH usually has a tremendous influence on CuNCs [46]. Therefore, the effects of pH on the fluorescence of BSA-CuNCs solution was investigated. The initial pH value of the prepared BSA-CuNCs was 8. As shown in Fig. 4 a, F450 of BSA-CuNCs gradually reduced as the pH value increased from 1 to 14. The fluorescence intensity of F620 was very weak in the pH range of 1–5 and 13–14, while it had a stable fluorescence intensity between 6 and 12. And the fluorescence of BSA-CuNCs solution in the pH range of 1–5 was blue under UV lamp, but it was red fluorescence in the pH range of 6–14 (Fig. 4b). Therefore, the visual pH monitoring can be realized by the fluorescence color changing from red to blue with the pH range of 6-5. As shown in Fig. 4c, when the pH was from 6 to 5, F450 gradually increases, while F620 gradually decreases, and the color changes from red to blue under UV lamp (Fig. 4d). Moreover, this change was irreversible. As shown in Fig. S7, when the pH was adjusted from 5 to 8, the red fluorescence signal did not recover. Therefore, the possible reason was that the structure of BSA-CuNCs was irreversibly broken in an acidic environment, which caused the fluorescence quenching of BSA-CuNCs. In order to verify this conjecture, the XRD of BSA-CuNCs at pH = 5 was measured. As shown in Fig. S8, it was found that the crystal morphology of BSA-CuNCs was destroyed entirely.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ratiometric fluorescence probe detection H2O2 based on BSA-CuNCs
ce:section.ce:para:
The initial pH value of BSA-CuNCs solution is 8 and BSA-CuNCs solution has a stable fluorescence in the neutral to weakly alkaline range. What's more, the pH value of actual samples such as serum is also in the neutral to weakly alkaline range. Therefore, the pH value of BSA-CuNCs is not specially adjusted in the application of detection. In the presence of many molecules and ions, the double emission fluorescence signals of BSA-CuNCs were studied. As shown in Fig. S6, these molecules and ions had little effect on the fluorescence of BSA-CuNCs except H2O2. It was noticeable that F450 displayed an obvious enhancement and F620 obvious decline in the presence of 1 mmol/L H2O2. In order to explore the best detection time for detecting H2O2, the incubation time after adding H2O2 was investigated. As shown in Fig. 5 , 2 min after the addition of H2O2, the fluorescence intensity of BSA-CuNCs solution no longer changes significantly. Therefore, the fluorescence spectrum was measured when the H2O2 was added for 2 min. Fig. 6 a exhibited the fluorescence spectra of BSA-CuNCs with various concentrations of H2O2. Results showed that F450 gradually increased with H2O2 concentration improvement, while that F620 gradually reduced. The ratio of F450/F620 against the concentration of H2O2 was illustrated in Fig. 6b, obeying equation (4) in the range of 0–100 μM.
(5) F450/F620 = 0.749 + 0.022C[H2O2]

The correlation coefficient was 0.996 (R2 = 0.996), and limit of detection for H2O2 was 0.082 μM calculated following the three times of the standard deviation of the blank signal. The normal intracellular concentration range of hydrogen peroxide is 0.01 μM–100 μM [3,47]. Therefore, the proposed probe has certain potential in practical biological application. It was noted that the proposed method had a lower detection limit and a wider detection range than previously reported methods by using other fluorescent materials (Table 2 ) [15,48–58]. Moreover, as the H2O2 concentration increased from 0 to 100 μM, the color change from red to blue was very easily identified by naked eyes under 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 6c). In order to test the anti-interference capability of BSA-CuNCs for H2O2 detection, the fluorescent response of BSA-CuNCs to 100 μM H2O2 in the presence of other substances was investigated. As shown in Fig. S9, the BSA-CuNCs had a superior anti-interference capability for H2O2 detection. A series of experimental results showed that the prepared BSA-CuNCs were suitable for ratiometric and visual detection of H2O2.

H2O2 can enhance F450 and decrease F620 of BSA-CuNCs. It can be seen from Fig. 3a that BSA-CuNCs had no obvious UV absorption peak at 450 nm, so there is no FRET between the emission of BSA at 450 nm and triplet emission of CuNCs at 620 nm. The possible mechanism was that H2O2 oxidized Cu0 to Cu2+, which destroyed the structure of BSA-CuNCs and released free BSA. This mechanism was verified by the Cu 2p XPS spectra of BSA-CuNCs after adding H2O2 (Fig. 6d), the fluorescence spectrum of BSA (Fig. S10a) and Cu(II)-BSA (Fig. S10b) after adding H2O2, and the fluorescence spectra of BSA and BSA-CuNCs (Fig. S10c). As shown in Fig. 6d, a new peak appears at 943.58 eV, which belongs to the characteristic peak of Cu2+, proving Cu0 was oxidized to Cu2+. Fig. S10a and Fig. S10b showed that the effect of H2O2 on the fluorescence of BSA and Cu(II)-BSA was negligible. Moreover, the emission of Cu(II)-BSA was at 473 nm instead of 450 nm. Therefore, the reason for the enhancement of 450 nm emission of BSA-CuNCs was neither due to Cu(II)-BSA nor to the interaction between BSA and H2O2. Fig. S10c showed that the fluorescence emission of BSA was higher than that of BSA-CuNCs at 450 nm. In conclusion, the fluorescence enhancement at 450 nm of BSA-CuNCs after adding H2O2 can be attributed to the formation of free BSA. The changed in the valence state of copper involved electron transfer, and the fluorescence quenching caused by electron transfer belongs to dynamic quenching [59]. The results in Fig. 3b showed that the fluorescence lifetime of BSA-CuNCs was 2.201 μs, while that of BSA-CuNCs/H2O2 was 1.900 μs? According to previous reports, the change of fluorescence lifetime indicated that the fluorescence quenching belongs to dynamic fluorescence quenching [60,61], which further proved the proposed mechanism.

For the detection of real biological samples, the cytotoxicity of fluorescent probes is a very important factor. Therefore, the cytotoxicity of BSA-CuNCs was evaluated by MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 7 , the results of MTT assay showed that the cell viability did not decrease significantly after adding BSA-CuNCs (up to 1000 μg/mL) into HeLa cells, indicating that BSA-CuNCs possessed low cytotoxicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of H2O2 in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further studied its applicability in real samples, the BSA-CuNCs were applied to detect H2O2 in rat serum sample. The studies were carried out with three concentrations (10, 20, 30 μM) spiked in rat serum sample. As shown in Table 3 , recoveries in the range of 96.252%–97.202% were obtained, indicating that the proposed method could be suitable for the detection of H2O2 in rat serum samples, and is expected to be applied to the detection of H2O2 in human serum.

In summary, a novel method for the rapid preparation of biocompatible red fluorescent copper nanoclusters (BSA-CuNCs) was proposed. The prepared BSA-CuNCs with dual emission peaks at 450 and 620 nm has been designed for ratiometric and visual detection H2O2 in the range of 0–100 μM. Compared with the traditional single signal fluorescent probe, the as-proposed dual emission ratio fluorescent probe had higher stability and sensitivity due to its self-calibration function. The work results suggested BSA-CuNCs can serve as a simple and quick fluorescent probe for the determination H2O2 in real samples.

Cunjin Wang: Roles/Writing - original draft; Min Yang: Conceptualization; Guohua Mi: Investigation; Bin Zhang: Investigation; Xiuhong Dou: Investigation; Enzhou Liu: Methodology; Xiaoyun Hu: Funding acquisition; Weiming Xue: Supervision; Jun Fan: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22078261 and 11974276), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2016M600809).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2021.109312.
